result.chapter_summary: Appendix III details the Grissmer study, the first experimental study to rigorously document the outsized impact of a knowledge-based curriculum on student literacy. By analyzing randomized kindergarten lotteries in Colorado, the study found that students in Core Knowledge schools achieved a 16-percentile-point boost in reading scores compared to their peers, suggesting that building knowledge is a critical, often overlooked lever for accelerating literacy.

claims.text: Literacy instruction in American schools has cycled between phonics and whole language for the last century.
claims.source_passage: Educators and researchers have been fighting the reading wars for the last century, with battles see-sawing literacy instruction in American schools from phonics to whole language and, most recently, back to phonics again.

claims.text: Reading scores in the United States have remained stagnant for more than a quarter-century.
claims.source_passage: Policymakers have entered the fray, after more than a quarter-century of stagnant reading scores in the United States.

claims.text: Thirty-two states and the District of Columbia have recently enacted 'science of reading' laws requiring evidence-based curricula.
claims.source_passage: Over the last decade, 32 states and the District of Columbia have adopted new "science of reading" laws that require schools to use curricula and instructional techniques that are deemed "evidence-based."

claims.text: Most current reading programs prioritize direct instruction in phonics and reading comprehension skills, like finding the main idea.
claims.source_passage: Such reading programs include direct instruction in phonics and reading comprehension skills, such as finding the main idea of a paragraph, and efforts to accelerate learning tend to double down on more of the same skill-building practice.

claims.text: A young child's knowledge of the social and physical world is a strong predictor of their academic success in elementary school.
claims.source_passage: For example, prior research by two of us found that a young child's knowledge of the social and physical world is a strong predictor of their academic success in elementary school.

claims.text: Students with high background knowledge of a topic outperform students with high general reading skills but low topic knowledge in comprehension tasks.
claims.source_passage: And advocates for knowledge-based education often cite the so-called "baseball study" where students reading a passage about baseball who knew about the sport were far better at understanding and summarizing the story than students who didn't, regardless of their general reading skills.

claims.text: The potential value of building student knowledge is frequently ignored in state and district efforts to improve reading instruction.
claims.source_passage: Yet the potential value of this approach is often an afterthought in state and district efforts to strengthen reading instruction

claims.text: Attending a Core Knowledge charter school provides outsized gains in science achievement, equivalent to 10 percentile points.
claims.source_passage: Students who attend a Core Knowledge charter also make outsized gains in science of 30 percent of a standard deviation, which is equivalent to a gain of 10 percentile points.

claims.text: The achievement gain from knowledge-based schooling is equivalent to the gap between mediocre U.S. reading performance and that of top nations like Singapore and Finland.
claims.source_passage: The size of this gain is approximately equivalent to the difference between the mediocre performance of U.S. 13-year-olds on the 2016 Progress in International Reading Literacy Study and that of top-scoring countries like Singapore and Finland.

claims.text: Unlike most reading interventions that have small, short-term effects, knowledge-based schooling produces large, long-term impacts.
claims.source_passage: Our results are also notable in their contrast with other studies of reading interventions, which typically find small, short-term effects.

claims.text: Current elementary school practices miss an opportunity to accelerate literacy by failing to build knowledge alongside component skills.
claims.source_passage: While the component skills of literacy are critical to student development and learning, our findings point to a missed opportunity to accelerate literacy by building knowledge at the same time.

claims.text: Skill building and knowledge accumulation are separate but complementary cognitive processes.
claims.source_passage: Skill building and knowledge accumulation are separate but complementary cognitive processes

claims.text: The combination of skill and knowledge produces a multiplicative effect on learning.
claims.source_passage: a fuller description of cognitive development could be "skill begets skill, knowledge begets knowledge, and skill combined with knowledge begets them both."

claims.text: A common base of shared knowledge is foundational for societal communication and the promotion of equal opportunity.
claims.source_passage: This "knowledge-based schooling" approach is rooted in the belief that a common base of shared knowledge is foundational for not just individual students' reading comprehension abilities but also for our ability as a society to communicate and promote equal opportunity.

claims.text: Roughly 20 percent of students in the Colorado charter lottery sample applied to multiple lotteries, typically two.
claims.source_passage: About one in five students in our sample applied to multiple charter lotteries usually two instead of one.

claims.text: Approximately 41 percent of applicants won at least one lottery for a Core Knowledge charter school, though only 47 percent of winners actually enrolled.
claims.source_passage: Some 41 percent won at least one lottery, and 47 percent of winners enrolled in that school.

claims.text: Attrition in standardized testing is higher among students who did not win the charter enrollment lottery compared to those who did.
claims.source_passage: the attrition rate for students who did not win the enrollment lottery is 5 percentage points higher than for lottery winners.

claims.text: Excluding the youngest lottery applicants and lotteries with high differential attrition prevents parental 'redshirting' and attrition bias from skewing study results.
claims.source_passage: To ensure that this attrition does not skew our results, we exclude from our analysis both the four lotteries with the highest rates of differential attrition between lottery winners and losers and the youngest applicants, who are more likely to be redshirted by their parents regardless of their lottery outcome.

claims.text: Students who attend Core Knowledge charter schools achieve significantly higher scores in reading, math, and science in grades 3-6 than comparable students not offered admission.
claims.source_passage: Several years after entering a Kindergarten charter-school enrollment lottery, students who won the lottery and attended a Core Knowledge school did better on reading, math, and science tests in grades 3-6 compared to comparable lottery entrants who were not offered admission.

claims.text: The educational gain from attending a Core Knowledge charter is equivalent to 16 percentile points in reading for a typical student.
claims.source_passage: Across grades 3-6, these students score 47 percent of a standard deviation higher in reading than comparable lottery applicants who did not have a chance to enroll. This is equivalent to a gain of 16 percentile points for a typical student.

claims.text: While Core Knowledge charters show positive effects on math performance, these gains fall short of statistical significance in the Colorado sample.
claims.source_passage: Effects in math are positive, at about 16 percent of a standard deviation, but fall short of statistical significance.

claims.text: Core Knowledge charter schools provide greater academic benefits for female students than for male students across all subjects.
claims.source_passage: Attending a Core Knowledge charter school increased learning in all subjects more for female than for male students.

claims.text: The achievement gains associated with Core Knowledge curriculum appear to stabilize by the 4th grade.
claims.source_passage: We also look at effects by student grade level and find no upward or downward trend, suggesting the effects may have stabilized by 4th grade

claims.text: Elevating knowledge to a central priority in education policy requires a conceptual shift because 'building knowledge' is not intuitively linked to achievement in the public mind.
claims.source_passage: elevating student knowledge to a more central place and higher priority in research and policy will require a significant conceptual shift—the term 'building knowledge' does not readily trigger a conceptual map linking the intervention to higher achievement

claims.text: Standardized national measures for elementary students should include well-designed assessments of student knowledge.
claims.source_passage: Well-designed measures of student knowledge should be considered as an important addition to other national measures for students in elementary grades.

claims.text: Efforts to define 'general knowledge' for public schools are likely to trigger political debate due to current climates of book banning and content control.
claims.source_passage: Any definition and measures of 'general knowledge' will need both scientific validity and political viability at a moment when attempts to ban library books and shape course content are on the rise.

claims.text: Building knowledge is a critical foundation for student literacy and produces potentially lifelong academic benefits.
claims.source_passage: However, the evidence points to building knowledge as a critical foundation of student literacy with potentially lifelong effects.

claims.text: Skillful reading and broad knowledge have potentially lifelong effects for students.
claims.source_passage:
potentially lifelong effects. The benefits of skillful reading and broad
knowledge

claims.text: Skillful reading and broad knowledge should be the shared starting point for education.
claims.source_passage: The benefits of skillful reading and broad knowledge should be a shared starting point

claims.text: A stronger approach to reading instruction can only grow from a foundation of broad knowledge.
claims.source_passage: from which a stronger approach to reading instruction can grow.

claims.text: Families in the Colorado study context have access to a variety of schooling options, including private schools and out-of-district public schools via open-enrollment laws.
claims.source_passage: These families generally have a range of schooling options, including private schools, other charter schools, and public schools outside their district under Colorado's open-enrollment law.

claims.text: Component skills of literacy are critical to student development and learning.
claims.source_passage: While the component skills of literacy are critical to student development and learning, our findings point to a missed opportunity to accelerate literacy by building knowledge at the same time.

claims.text: Approximately 36 percent of students in the Colorado study sample failed to complete all scheduled PARCC tests through grade 6.
claims.source_passage: However, roughly 36 percent of students in our sample did not complete all scheduled PARCC tests through grade 6

claims.text: Test-score attrition in the study was caused by student relocation, transfers to other schools, homeschooling, and exemptions for language learners or special-education students.
claims.source_passage: First, some students stop participating in Colorado's PARCC testing because they move out of state, transfer to a different school, or are homeschooled. A second group of students don't have test-score data because they are exempted as language learners or special-education students.

claims.text: A full description of cognitive development involves a tripartite relationship: skill begets skill, knowledge begets knowledge, and skill combined with knowledge begets them both.
claims.source_passage: Skill building and knowledge accumulation are separate but complementary cognitive processes, and while the adage 'skill begets skill' may be true, a fuller description of cognitive development could be 'skill begets skill, knowledge begets knowledge, and skill combined with knowledge begets them both.'

claims.text: Any proposed definition or measure of 'general knowledge' for schools requires scientific validity in addition to political viability.
claims.source_passage: Any definition and measures of "general knowledge" will need both scientific validity and political viability at a moment when attempts to ban library books and shape course content are on the rise.

claims.text: The reading achievement gain found in the study is specifically compared to the performance levels of U.S. 13-year-olds on the 2016 Progress in International Reading Literacy Study (PIRLS).
claims.source_passage: The size of this gain is approximately equivalent to the difference between the mediocre performance of U.S. 13-year-olds on the 2016 Progress in International Reading Literacy Study and that of top-scoring countries like Singapore and Finland.

evidence.description: The Grissmer Study: An experimental study using randomized kindergarten lotteries in nine oversubscribed Colorado charter schools to measure long-term reading outcomes.
The 'baseball study' showing topic knowledge outweighs general reading skills in comprehension.
evidence.source_reference: Recht and Leslie (often cited as 'baseball study')

evidence.description: 2016 Progress in International Reading Literacy Study (PIRLS) data comparing U.S. scores to Singapore and Finland.
Prior research by the authors showing early knowledge of the social/physical world predicts elementary success.
Analysis of PARCC reading, math, and science test scores in grades 3-6 for lottery winners vs. losers.
A natural experiment utilizing kindergarten enrollment lotteries in Colorado charter schools to ensure random assignment.
Longitudinal data tracking students over a period of up to seven years of schooling.
Statistical adjustment for student gender, race, ethnicity, and eligibility for free or reduced-price lunch.
A study on the impact of background knowledge on student literacy outcomes and its long-term benefits.
evidence.source_reference: Grissmer, D., et al. (2024). Education Next.
evidence.source_passage:
This article appeared in the Spring 2024 issue of Education
Next.

concepts.author_definition: Reading programs using materials and activities based on a sequence of integrated science and social studies topics, texts, and vocabulary.
Curricula and techniques deemed evidence-based, currently often focusing on direct instruction in phonics and component skills.
concepts.common_alternative: Sometimes used broadly to include knowledge-building, but often narrowed to just phonics in legislative practice.

concepts.author_definition: An approach rooted in the belief that a planned sequence of shared knowledge is foundational for individual comprehension and societal equity.
The practice of delaying a child's entry into kindergarten, often to provide an age-based advantage, which can skew educational data if not controlled for.
A measurable set of facts and concepts that all public-school students should know, serving as the basis for literacy.
The essential prerequisite of background knowledge required for reading comprehension and effective literacy skills.
The common baseline of broad knowledge and reading skills that all students should acquire to facilitate further educational growth.
A century-long conflict in literacy instruction alternating between phonics and whole language approaches.
A cognitive link that allows an intervention (like 'building knowledge') to be intuitively associated with an outcome (like achievement).
The variance in dropout rates or missing data between the treatment group (lottery winners) and the control group (lottery losers).
Progress in International Reading Literacy Study; an international assessment used to benchmark national reading performance.

cases.name: Colorado Core Knowledge Charters
cases.description: Nine oversubscribed charter schools in Colorado that held random lotteries for kindergarten in 2009-11. The study compared 475 winners who enrolled to 1,356 losers to determine the impact of the Core Knowledge curriculum.
key_passages: To assess the impact of the Core Knowledge curriculum on student achievement, we look at nine oversubscribed Colorado charter schools that all use the curriculum, had been open for at least four years, and held random enrollment lotteries

cases.name: Colorado Core Knowledge Charter Study
cases.description: A study of 475 lottery winners and 1,356 lottery losers in Colorado Core Knowledge charter schools. The study tracked performance on PARCC tests through grade 6, finding substantial gains for those in the knowledge-building curriculum, particularly in reading and science, and notably stronger effects for female students.
key_passages: In all, 475 lottery winners went on to attend a Core Knowledge charter, while 1,356 students did not win the lottery and attended school elsewhere.
Across grades 3-6, these students score 47 percent of a standard deviation higher in reading than comparable lottery applicants who did not have a chance to enroll.

cases.name: The Grissmer Study (Education Next 2024)
cases.description: A collaborative research effort involving scholars from the University of Virginia, Notre Dame, and Auburn University examining the foundational role of knowledge in literacy.
key_passages: This article appeared in the Spring 2024 issue of Education Next.
David Grissmer is research professor in the School of Education & Human Development at the University of Virginia...

thinkers.name: E.D. Hirsch, Jr.
thinkers.key_idea: Knowledge-building education and the Core Knowledge curriculum.
thinkers.engagement: The study validates Hirsch's theoretical claims about the necessity of a shared knowledge base for literacy and equity.
key_passages: The Core Knowledge curriculum was created in the 1980s by E.D. Hirsch, Jr., a researcher and advocate of knowledge-building education.

thinkers.name: David Grissmer
thinkers.key_idea: Knowledge-building curricula delivered in a charter school setting significantly improve standardized test performance in reading and science over the long term.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch includes this study as an appendix to provide empirical validation for the book's core thesis regarding the necessity of a knowledge-based curriculum.
key_passages: David Grissmer is research professor in the School of Education & Human Development at the University of Virginia... This article appeared in the Spring 2024 issue of Education Next.

thinkers.name: Daniel T. Willingham
thinkers.key_idea: The psychological and cognitive benefits of broad knowledge as a foundation for reading and learning.
thinkers.engagement: Willingham is cited as a co-author of the Grissmer study, lending cognitive psychology expertise to the empirical findings.
key_passages: Daniel T. Willingham is professor of psychology at the University of Virginia.

actors.name: Grissmer, D.
actors.context: Lead researcher of the multi-year study in Colorado.

actors.name: Core Knowledge Foundation
actors.context: Organization providing the curriculum used in the study schools.

actors.name: University of Virginia School of Education & Human Development
actors.context: The primary institutional home for the researchers conducting the Grissmer study.

actors.name: Education Next
actors.context: The journal that published the Grissmer study results in Spring 2024.

actors.name: David Grissmer
actors.context: Lead researcher of the study validating knowledge-based literacy.

actors.name: Daniel T. Willingham
actors.context: Cognitive psychologist and collaborator on the Grissmer study.

actors.name: Education Next
actors.context: The publication venue for the findings of the knowledge study.

objections_raised.objection: To accelerate literacy, schools should focus primarily on doubling down on phonics and reading comprehension skill-building.
objections_raised.response: This is a missed opportunity; experimental data shows that building knowledge at the same time as skills produces significantly larger and longer-term reading gains.

objections_raised.objection: Student attrition (moving, homeschooling, exemptions) might bias the results of the lottery study.
objections_raised.response: The researchers excluded lotteries with high differential attrition and controlled for demographic variables and 'redshirting' to ensure validity.

objections_raised.objection: Defining 'general knowledge' is politically impossible in a climate of book bans and curriculum wars.
objections_raised.response: While politically challenging, scientific validity and the proven benefits of knowledge for literacy make it a necessary starting point for reform.
